title: Economic Growth & Resilience
abstract: Abstract This chapter looks at the overall macroeconomic and social situation of the CEEE at the onset of Russia’s aggression on Ukraine and over the consecutive 18 months. Given its close proximity to the Covid-19 crisis, we interpret these shocks together as a twin crisis and draw parallels with the 2008-12 Global Financial Crisis (GFC). Unlike during the GFC, most of the CEEE seems to have recovered from the first phase (the Covid-19 shock) quickly. Under the surface, one can nevertheless discover important vulnerabilities and imbalances, in energy prices and supply security, the build-up of inflationary pressures and a decreased fiscal space. Though the status of the resilience capacities of the CEEE in early 2022 was still better than before the GFC, it was somewhat weaker than before the Covid-19 shock. Moreover, through their fossil energy imports, the CEEE had a substantial direct exposure to Russia. At the onset of the Ukraine shock, there were widespread fears of catastrophic consequences for industry, households in the winter, and further massive increases in energy prices. Such doomsday scenarios did not materialise, as European countries reacted more flexibly to both the quantity and price shock than anticipated. While in the majority of the CEEE the Ukrainian war lead to either a recession or a slowdown of the recovery from Covid, the recession is fairly shallow and seems to be mostly transitory. Overall, the main consequence of the war seems to be inflation, with a heterogeneous impact on the population. This may increase social pressures further, and coupled with increased societal polarisation, it may jeopardise the green transition. Finally, the war will have important geopolitical repercussions. With stronger geoeconomic and geopolitical competition, globalisation patterns may change, which will surely affect the CEEE that are highly integrated into the world economy.
summary_en: How well did Central and Eastern European economies (CEEE) hold up through the twin shock of COVID-19 and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine? Benczúr and Kónya frame the two crises as a single 'twin' shock and compare the CEEE response to the 2008–12 Global Financial Crisis. CEEEs recovered from the COVID shock quickly, but with vulnerabilities under the surface — energy-supply exposure to Russia, building inflationary pressures, reduced fiscal space. The doomsday scenarios for the 2022 winter didn't materialise, partly because Europe responded more flexibly than expected. The dominant lasting consequence is heterogeneous inflation, with social-polarisation risks for the green transition.
summary_hu: Mennyire álltak helyt a közép- és kelet-európai gazdaságok (KKE) a COVID-19 és Oroszország 2022-es ukrajnai inváziójának dupla sokkjában? Benczúr és Kónya 'iker' sokkként keretezi a két válságot, és összehasonlítja a KKE-választ a 2008–12-es globális pénzügyi válsággal. A KKE-k gyorsan helyreálltak a COVID-sokkból, de a felszín alatt sebezhetőségek voltak — energiaellátási kitettség Oroszország felé, épülő inflációs nyomások, csökkenő fiskális mozgástér. A 2022-es télre vonatkozó katasztrófaforgatókönyvek nem valósultak meg, részben mert Európa rugalmasabban reagált a vártnál. A meghatározó tartós következmény a heterogén infláció, a zöld átmenetre nézve társadalmi-polarizációs kockázatokkal.
data_used: Macroeconomic and trade data for the CEEE region, ~2018–2023. Energy-import exposure measures, fiscal indicators, inflation breakdowns. Comparative analysis against 2008–12 GFC reaction.
data_used_hu: Makrogazdasági és kereskedelmi adatok a KKE-régióra, ~2018–2023. Energia-import-kitettség mértékek, fiskális mutatók, infláció felbontás. Összehasonlító elemzés a 2008–12-es GFC-reakcióval szemben.
policy_relevance: Direct input for MNB and NGM long-run macro-policy planning post-2022. The chapter argues for: (i) faster build-up of fiscal buffers in good times so they're available for the next twin-shock; (ii) explicit policy attention to the energy-supply / inflation / social-polarisation chain that could derail the green transition; (iii) careful monitoring of heterogeneous inflation impact across income brackets. Useful framing for the EU Recovery Fund implementation in CEEEs.
policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlen input az MNB és az NGM 2022 utáni hosszú távú makropolitikai tervezéséhez. A fejezet érvel: (i) gyorsabb fiskális puffer-építés jó időkben, hogy elérhetők legyenek a következő iker-sokkban; (ii) explicit szakpolitikai figyelem az energiaellátás / infláció / társadalmi-polarizáció láncolatára, amely eltérítheti a zöld átmenetet; (iii) a heterogén inflációs hatás gondos monitorozása jövedelmi csoportokon át. Hasznos keret az EU Helyreállítási Alap KKE-ben való végrehajtásához.
